The Collection

Crystal Tableware and Serving Pieces

Every celebration begins not with the meal itself, but with the way the table is set. A beautifully arranged table transforms an ordinary family dinner, a gathering of friends, or a festive occasion into a memorable experience. Crystal has a unique ability to fill a space with light, elegance, and a sense of timeless luxury.

The collection of crystal serving pieces from the museum of the Pervomaysky Glass Factory includes artist-designed salad bowls, fruit bowls, candy dishes, serving platters, divided serving dishes, and decorative vessels created during the 1970s and 1980s by the factory's leading designers and master craftsmen. These objects are distinguished by harmonious proportions, the exceptional quality of their crystal, and masterfully executed deep hand-cut decoration, elevating each piece into a work of decorative art.

Using such objects is a pleasure in itself. Serving fresh fruit in a monumental crystal bowl, presenting a salad in a brilliantly cut crystal dish, or offering chocolates and sweets from an elegant hand-crafted crystal candy bowl transforms even the simplest food into a refined experience. As light passes through the deep-cut facets, the crystal comes alive with sparkling reflections, giving every table setting a festive and sophisticated character.

The artists of the Pervomaysky Glass Factory designed these objects not merely as functional tableware but as integral elements of interior culture and the art of hospitality. Every form was carefully proportioned to harmonize with other serving pieces while possessing the sculptural presence of an independent work of art.

Today, these crystal serving pieces are increasingly appreciated by collectors, interior designers, and admirers of twentieth-century decorative arts. They recall a time when beauty was an essential part of everyday life and when respect for guests was expressed through a carefully prepared table and exquisitely crafted crystal.

Each object in this collection preserves the traditions of one of the Soviet Union's finest schools of artistic glassmaking. Together, they demonstrate that true luxury lies not in excess, but in exceptional craftsmanship, enduring quality, and the timeless culture of gracious dining.

A pair of crystal dishes with star and diamond hobstar cutting, one larger with a scalloped rim — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Crystal salad or fruit bowl with looping hand-cut ribbon decoration and a scalloped rim — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Footed crystal compote with diamond lattice cutting — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Round crystal dish with a radiating pinwheel-cut design — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Scalloped crystal serving dish with a diamond-cut center and petal-shaped rim panels — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Footed crystal compote with fan and leaf-motif cutting — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Footed crystal compote with fluted panels and a hand-engraved floral motif — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Round scalloped crystal serving plate with hobnail-cut center and a ribbon-patterned border — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Crystal vessel with satin-frosted vertical panels and a band of applied bubble decoration — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, museum collection.

Oval crystal serving dish with star and diamond cutting and a scalloped rim — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Rectangular crystal serving tray with diagonal ribbed cutting — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Fan-shaped footed crystal dish with a textured, clustered cut pattern — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Footed crystal compote with diamond-cut bowl and an octagonal faceted foot — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Footed crystal compote with diamond-cut bowl on a baluster stem — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Oval scalloped crystal serving dish with fluted panel decoration — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.

Asymmetric octagonal crystal dish with a star-cut center and ribbed border — Pervomaysky Glass Factory, 1970s–1980s.
